desks.title: Best budget gaming desks for small UK rooms
desks.intro: Every existing UK gaming-desk guide we found covers the products fine but treats "small room" as an afterthought bolted onto a generic top-10 list. This one starts from the actual constraint UK gamers have: box bedrooms, shared spaces, desks that need to double as a workspace.

sections.heading: Best overall value: IKEA Utespelare — ~£129
sections.body: A proper gaming desk (not a repurposed office desk) sized sensibly for UK rooms, solid build quality at the price, widely stocked so it's easy to actually buy rather than waiting on a container ship.

sections.heading: Best true-budget option: IKEA LAGKAPTEN + ADILS — ~£55-£65
sections.body: Not marketed as a "gaming desk" at all, which is exactly why it works in a small room — flat, sturdy, no wasted RGB-branded bulk. The honest pick for anyone who wants surface area over aesthetics.

sections.heading: Best for a corner/box room: HOMIDEC L-Shaped — ~£79-£99
sections.body: L-shaped desks use a room's corner instead of eating a whole wall, which matters more in a UK bedroom than in the sprawling US setups most gaming-desk content is written for.

sections.heading: Best for storage-starved rooms: Coleshome — ~£89-£119
sections.body: If the room has nowhere else to put headsets, controllers, or cables, this prioritises built-in storage over raw surface area.

sections.heading: Worth calling out: ELEGANT Gaming Desk — ~£68
sections.body: Budget RGB option if look matters as much as function — flagged honestly as style-over-substance rather than pretending it's the outright best pick.

sections.heading: What we won't do
sections.body: We're not going to recommend a "gaming desk" purely because it has a cup holder and a headphone hook molded into the corner. Every pick above earns its place on actual dimensions, stability, and fit for a small room — not branding.

sources: Cross-referenced current UK retail listings and 2026 desk-buying-guide coverage across multiple outlets, not a single source.

chairs.title: Best gaming chairs for small UK rooms
chairs.intro: Most gaming chair guides assume a sprawling setup. In a UK box room, footprint and a folding footrest matter as much as ergonomics.

sections.heading: Best overall: Racingreat Ergonomic Gaming Chair — sub-£55
sections.body: Compact seat and base footprint, no bulky footrest, adjustable seat height, reclining backrest, headrest pillow and lumbar cushion at the price.

sections.heading: Best value with a footrest: Symino Gaming Chair
sections.body: Feature-rich with footrest and full lumbar support — the footrest folds away when not in use, which matters in a tight room.

sections.heading: Best for the smallest bedrooms: bigzzia Gaming Chair — under £50
sections.body: No footrest, which keeps both the price and the footprint down. The honest pick when the room simply won't take anything bigger.

sections.heading: Best solid build under £90: SONGMICS Racing Gaming Chair OBG073BH08
sections.body: The most solid construction found under £90 in current UK comparisons.

sources: Cross-referenced current UK retail listings and 2026 chair-buying-guide coverage across multiple outlets.

monitor-arms.title: Best monitor arms and cable management for small desks
monitor-arms.intro: On a desk under 120cm wide, a monitor arm frees more usable surface than almost any other single purchase.

sections.heading: Best overall: Ergotron LX — ~£130
sections.body: Smooth gas-spring movement, 25kg load capacity, fits monitors up to 34in. Cables route through channels in the arm secured by detachable cladding. The arm 90% of UK buyers should get.

sections.heading: Design-conscious alternative: Herman Miller Flo Plus
sections.body: Ultra-clean appearance with cable channels integrated into the design, 12-year warranty. A premium pick, not a starting recommendation.

sections.heading: If a full arm isn't in budget yet
sections.body: A cable tray, cable spine, and a monitor riser is often steadier than a heavy arm balanced on a lightweight desk anyway — worth doing in that order rather than stretching for the arm first.

sources: Cross-referenced current UK retail listings and 2026 monitor-arm buying-guide coverage.

cable-management.title: Cable management for small desks
cable-management.intro: In a small room, trailing cables are the first thing you see walking in. This is the cheapest, highest-impact category on the whole list.

sections.heading: Best starting point: a single under-desk cable tray — ~£15-£25
sections.body: Budget mesh trays start around £15; premium retractable versions run closer to £25. This catches the power strip and bulk cabling in one go.

sections.heading: Best value: a complete kit rather than piecing it together — ~£28-£35
sections.body: Buying a tray, ties, clips, and sleeves separately adds up to roughly £46 in total; bundled kits covering the same ground typically run £28-35.

sections.heading: Worth adding once the basics are sorted
sections.body: Adhesive cable clips running down the desk leg to the floor socket — a few pounds, and the difference between "tidy" and "actually tidy."

sources: Cross-referenced current UK retail listings and 2026 cable-management comparison coverage.

lighting.title: Desk and bias lighting for gaming setups
lighting.intro: Bias lighting behind a monitor measurably reduces eye strain, separate from whatever aesthetic value RGB adds.

sections.heading: Best budget pick: RGBIC-style strip lights — ~£15-£28
sections.body: Enough for a bias-lighting effect behind a monitor without turning the whole room into a light show — useful in a small room where a fully-lit RGB setup can feel overwhelming rather than atmospheric.

sections.heading: Best mid-range: Govee Strip Light 2 Pro — ~£100
sections.body: Wireless control, wide colour range, syncs with Corsair iCUE if the rest of the setup is Corsair gear. A "once you know you want this hobby" purchase, not a starting recommendation.

sections.heading: The actual useful bit, regardless of budget
sections.body: Bias lighting behind the monitor at roughly 300-500 lumens per metre measurably reduces eye strain by softening the contrast between a bright screen and a dark room.

sources: Cross-referenced current UK retail listings and 2026 desk-lighting buying-guide coverage.

mini-pcs.title: Best mini PCs for tight rooms
mini-pcs.intro: This category moves fast — mini PC pricing and specs shift with component cycles, so treat this as a starting shortlist to check current pricing against, not a fixed table.

sections.heading: Best overall for a small room: Geekom AX8 Pro
sections.body: Genuinely small footprint, real gaming capability (not just a media-box PC pretending to game), sensible price for what it does.

sections.heading: Step-up option: GEEKOM A9 Max
sections.body: Higher-end components in a still-compact chassis; handles popular titles like Fortnite, Valorant, and FIFA-style games comfortably at 1080p. Worth it if the room genuinely can't fit a tower.

sections.heading: Honest caution — budget option: Blackview MP200
sections.body: Good value for general use, but flagged plainly: no dedicated graphics card and weak cooling means it will disappoint anyone expecting real gaming performance.

sections.heading: Premium: Corsair One i500
sections.body: For someone with real budget and a genuine small-space constraint, not a compromise pick.

sources: Cross-referenced current UK/US retail listings and 2026 mini-PC buying-guide coverage. Specific pricing changes fast in this category — always check live price.
